narrative: SAIL’s FY2023 ESG profile reflected the strengths and constraints of a large, public-sector, integrated steelmaker. It ranked first on 28 indicators but fourth on 31, producing a mid-pack overall position. Environmentally, SAIL’s legacy BF-BOF and captive/process-energy footprint weighed heavily: it was the laggard on physical GHG intensity at 2.8 tCO2e/tcs, performed weakly on per-rupee Scope 1 and 2 intensity, and ranked last on energy physical intensity because the standardised value was missing. It also lacked comparable SOx, NOx and PM disclosures in the table, weakening its air-emissions benchmark. At the same time, SAIL showed strengths in construction and demolition waste, e-waste, low waste disposal and MSME sourcing. Socially, SAIL was comparatively strong on worker and employee LTIFR and was the only company with female KMP representation in FY2023, but it lagged on worker fatalities and training breadth outside worker H&S. Its 100% sustainable sourcing and leading MSME sourcing reflected public-sector procurement orientation and vendor-development obligations. Financially, SAIL remained a major scale player, with turnover above ₹104,000 crore, but below Tata and JSW. The strategic picture is of a large domestic steel institution with strong procurement reach and some safety strengths, but constrained by older integrated assets, energy intensity, uneven disclosure completeness and carbon-heavy process architecture.
